original_abstract: Bacterial wound infections remain a global health crisis due to the rapid rise of multidrug-resistant pathogens. In this study, we developed an innovative, antibiotic-free antimicrobial strategy using bimetallic hybrid nanozymes. These nanozymes (FMA NPs) were engineered by growing ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) in situ onto a metal-organic framework (MOF) shell that stabilizes a magnetite (Fe3O4) core. The resulting hybrid structure exhibits powerful synergistic peroxidase (POD)-like activity, enabling the generation of high levels of toxic hydroxyl radicals (·OH) even at physiological concentrations of H2O2 (0.97 mM). Our results demonstrate that FMA NPs possess excellent biocompatibility and exert potent antibacterial effects against both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria, effectively disrupting established biofilms. In vivo animal models further confirmed that the hybrid nanozymes significantly accelerate wound healing with a high degree of safety and no detectable systemic toxicity. This well-designed nanocatalytic platform represents a promising antibiotic-independent therapy for managing infected wounds in clinical settings.
humanized_abstract: Bacterial wound infections remain an urgent global health challenge because of the increasing number of multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogens. This study for the first time developed a simple and antibiotic-free antimicrobial approach by using antibiotic-resistant bimetallic hybrid nanozymes. Ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) was synthesized in situ on the MOF shell, forming a bimetallic hybrid nanozyme framework-metal-organic framework-magnetite (FMA NPs). FMA NPs showed synergistic peroxidase-like activity (POD) and produced large amounts of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) under a low physiological concentration of H2O2 (0.97 mM). FMA NPs also showed good biocompatibility and strong antibacterial activities against both Gram-negative Escherichia coli and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus bacteria and their biofilms. In addition, an animal experiment showed that the hybrid nanozymes had excellent promotion effects on wound healing and safe for use on animals without showing any significant systemic toxicities. Thus, Bacterial wound infections remain an urgent global health challenge because of the increasing number of multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogens. This study for the first time developed a simple and antibiotic-free antimicrobial approach by using antibiotic-resistant bimetallic hybrid nanozymes. Ultra-small gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) was synthesized in situ on the MOF shell, forming a bimetallic hybrid nanozyme framework-metal-organic framework-magnetite (FMA NPs). FMA NPs showed synergistic peroxidase-like activity (POD) and produced large amounts of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) under a low physiological concentration of H2O2 (0.97 mM). FMA NPs also showed good biocompatibility and strong antibacterial activities against both Gram-negative Escherichia coli and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus bacteria and their biofilms. In addition, an animal experiment showed that the hybrid nanozymes had excellent promotion effects on wound healing and safe for use on animals without showing any significant systemic toxicities. This nanocatalytic strategy provides a new idea for developing a potentially effective antibiotic-free therapy for wound infections.
